Name: Smallest Low Period
Description: Shows spaceships of all periods < 32 for which examples are known. Minimum populations are listed below, each spaceship having been chosen to minimize this minimum population. (Often there are small variations of the given spaceship that have the same minimum population.) Spaceships are arranged in period order, reading right then down. Gaps between spaceships do not correlate with gaps in the known spaceship periods -- the extra space just prevents fast higher- period spaceships from crashing into slow lower-period ones. Period Min population ------ -------------- p2   64 p3   25 p4    5 p5   30 p6   56 p7   20 p8   31 p9   55 p10   28 p12   39 p14   174 p15   112 p16   28 p18   138 p20   32 p21   176 p22   213 p24   39 p25   165 p26   187 p27   185 p28   185 p30   128 p2: Dean Hickerson, 28 Jul 1989. One of the first p2 spaceships. p3: Dean Hickerson, Aug 1989. The first known p3 spaceship. A p3 spaceship found later by David Bell has the same minimum population, but a larger maximum one (see c3-orthogonal.rle). 25 has been proven to be the minimum population for p3 ships. p4: "glider" by Richard Guy, 1970. The first known spaceship. p5: Paul Tooke, 7 Dec 2000. p6: Hartmut Holzwart, 19 May 2009. The ship was found by looking for symmetric support for the two wings, which were found by Paul Tooke in Mar 2006. p7: Josh Ball's "loafer", 17 Feb 2013. In terms of minimum population, this is tied as the fifth-smallest known spaceship. p8: Jason Summers, 29 Oct 2000. This was originally found as a p4 ship with a loosely-connected back end. Removing this back end results in the p8 ship shown here. p9: David Bell found the first p9 spaceship in May 1992, by deleting the back part of one of his p3 spaceships. The smaller one shown here was found by Paul Tooke, 7 Jun 2001. p10: "copperhead" by 'zdr', 5 Mar 2016. p12: There is a tie here between two very different c/2 spaceships. Not shown is the Schick engine (Paul Schick, 1972) which, however, can be seen in the p24 example below. The one actually shown is by Mark Niemiec, March 1997. It's a Coe ship (see p16 below) perturbed by a MWSS. p14: Paul Tooke found the first p14 spaceship, 19 Dec 2001. The one shown here is a much smaller one (with the same p14 part) that he found on 28 Jan 2002, except that I've changed the p2 spaceship at the front to save a few more cells. p15: This ship is based on a p15 puffer discovered by Paul Tooke in Oct 2002. A smaller p3 support was found by Matthias Merzenich on 8 Sep 2015. p16: "Coe ship" by Tim Coe, 26 Oct 1995. Coe found this by dismembering a p4 spaceship he had just found. p18: This p18 spaceship by Jason Summers, 2 Dec 2000, is the result of welding his p6 spaceship to a p9 spaceship by David Bell. This spaceship is trivial in the sense that it has no p18 part, merely disjoint p6 and p9 parts. There are known nontrivial p18 ships, but they are all larger than this trivial example. p20: Jason Summers, 8 Apr 2006. A glider suppresses the output of a c/2 puffer. p21: Paul Tooke found the first p21 spaceship on 14 Jan 2002. The one shown here is a smaller one found by Matthias Merzenich on 26 Sep 2015. p22: Paul Tooke, 27 Oct 2003. p24: A simple derivative of the Schick engine. p25: The first p25 spaceship was found by Paul Tooke on 8 Feb 2002. The one shown here is a much smaller one he found later the same month. p26: David Bell's 30 Apr 2001 version of Paul Tooke's p78 puffer. p27: This is based on the first known p27 spaceship, which was found by Paul Tooke in Oct 2002. The p3 support was reduced by David Bell in Sep 2003. p28: Stephen Silver, 28 Jan 2002, based on Paul Tooke's p14 ship. p30: This is a pre-pulsar pulled by two spiders, found by David Bell on 30 May 1998. The variant obtained by moving one side forward two spaces has the same minimum population. (This variant was noticed by Alan Hensel.) small_p.rle from Stephen Silver's 12 Jan 2004 'ships' collection. Updated on 1 May 2018.

Name: Spacefiller
Description: A small spacefiller. Spacefillers are the fastest-growing known pattern in Conway's Game of Life (probably the fastest possible). They fill space to a density of 1/2, conjectured to be the maximum density, and they do it at a speed of c/2 in each of the 4 directions, which has been proven to be the maximum possible speed. This pattern starts with 200 cells, not the record lowest number of starting cells for a spacefiller (at the time of this writing, the record is 187). Quadratic-growth patterns that start with as few as 26 ON cells are now known, but their growth rate is comparatively slow. The population of spacefillers are easy to compute. This one's equations are: [(t+17)^2+511]/4 for t divisible by 4; [(t+17)^2+608]/4 for t mod 4 = 1; [(t+17)^2+563]/4 for t mod 4 = 2; [(t+17)^2+580]/4 for t mod 4 = 3. Most spacefillers at this time have p2 stretchers on the left and right instead of the flipper stretchers in this pattern. Original idea and middle part by Al Hensel; original construction and top/bottom stretchers by Hartmut Holzwart; size optimization by David Bell, Al Hensel, and Tim Coe. This spacefiller by Hartmut Holzwart, 4 Nov 1998. From Alan Hensel's "lifebc" pattern collection.

Name: Spaceship Types
Description: A period p spaceship which displaces itself (m,n) during its period, where m >= n, is said to be of type (m,n)c/p. It can be shown that p >= 2m+2n. The number of possible types of spaceship of period p is therefore [k(k+4)/4], where k = [p/2]. (Here, [x] denotes the floor of x, i.e., the greatest integer not exceeding x.) Note, however, that no-one has ever proved that all these "possible" types really are possible, so the above formula may only be an upper bound. The following table shows the mininum number of cells for spaceships of all types with period < 10. Those marked [*] are known to be best possible. Goucher    6 Mar 2018 (3,0)c/6 86 cells        Matthias Merzenich  4 Feb 2012 (1,0)c/7 20 cells        Josh Ball      17 Feb 2013 (1,1)c/7 83 cells        Matthias Merzenich  19 Aug 2011 (2,0)c/7 36 cells        David Eppstein    12 Jan 2000 (2,1)c/7 (no known example) (3,0)c/7 702 cells        Tim Coe       11 Jun 2016 (1,0)c/8 (no known example) (1,1)c/8 (no known example) (2,0)c/8 74 cells        Josh Ball        Mar 2010 (2,1)c/8 (no known example) (2,2)c/8 117 cells        Jason Summers     2 Dec 2000 (3,0)c/8 (no known example) (3,1)c/8 (no known example) (4,0)c/8 31 cells        Jason Summers    29 Oct 2000 (1,0)c/9 (no known example) (1,1)c/9 (no known example) (2,0)c/9 (no known example) (2,1)c/9 (no known example) (2,2)c/9 (no known example) (3,0)c/9 55 cells        Paul Tooke      7 Jun 2001 (3,1)c/9 (no known example) (4,0)c/9 (no known example) types.rle from Stephen Silver's 12 Jan 2004 'ships' collection. Name: Spider Rake
Description: p360 Spider Rake Nine gliders travel in a figure-8 loop, trapped by c/5 spiders, liberating one glider each time. The gliders in the loop collide with the prepulsar part of a c/5 spider combo to form a Herschel traveling downwards. The Herschel is guided by other spiders, producing a glider with the correct parity to form a large period spaceship - in this case, p3240, which can be divided by 9 to form a p360 spaceship. The Herschel on the left side is then killed, but the Herschel on the right side is pushed a little further to liberate one more glider before it is killed. On the far right side, another c/5 spider-prepulsar spaceship reflects the forward gliders, making this a backward rake. By David Bell, June 3, 1998 From Alan Hensel's "lifebc" pattern collection.

Name: Traffic Light Hasslers
Description: Traffic-light hasslers. There is an unusual proliferation of oscillators engineered this way, because there are so many ways to perturb a traffic light in such a way that it reappears somewhere nearby. This collection is split into two parts: The top contains standard traffic light hasslers. The bottom contains "pre-pulsar" hasslers. A pre-pulsar is a pair of pre-traffic lights whose centers are 5 cells apart. If left to evolve, they would not form traffic lights, but would instead form the period-3 pulsar oscillator. From Alan Hensel's "lifebc" pattern collection. Updated on 1 May 2018.

If Golly has enough memory available, this pattern will run faster at 8^4 or 8^5 after finishing its first construction cycle, because many of the hashtiles will be re-used on future cycles. http://www.conwaylife.com/forums/viewtopic.php?p=44384#p44377
